one_line_takeaway: Across jailbreak prompting, unsupervised activation probing (CCS/Cluster-Norm), and Automatic Prompt Engineering for detecting MovieLens-1M memorization in LLaMA-1B/3B, only APE yields practically useful (though still partial, peaking at 18-26% exact-match) extraction of memorized item titles, while all three methods fail to recover memorized numerical user/rating fields, and the probing methods' 'success' on titles cannot be conclusively distinguished from generic linguistic plausibility judgment.
key_finding: Table 3 (CCS/Cluster-Norm balanced accuracy vs. random=0.50): movies.dat -- CCS 0.92, Cluster-Norm 0.94; users.dat -- CCS 0.51, Cluster-Norm 0.52; ratings.dat -- CCS 0.53, Cluster-Norm 0.51 -- i.e. near-perfect separation of real vs. fabricated movie titles but no better than chance on numerical user/rating fields. Table 4 (APE exact-match accuracy by temperature): for LLaMA-1B item extraction, accuracy rose from 8% (T=0.1) to a peak of 18.1% (T=0.9), then fell to 1.1% (T=2.0); for LLaMA-3B item extraction it peaked at 26% at T=0.7 (25% at T=0.9), also falling off at extreme temperatures; user and rating extraction stayed below 0.1% ('-') for both model sizes at every temperature. The paper reports these APE item-extraction peaks (18.1% for LLaMA-1B, 26% for LLaMA-3B) as exceeding the item-coverage baseline from Di Palma et al. (2025), whose own reported baseline numbers (reproduced in the same Table 4 for comparison) were 1.93% item / 10.98% user / 6.49% rating for LLaMA-1B-class models and 2.68% item / 13.26% user / 6.22% rating for LLaMA-3B-class models. Jailbreak prompting produced only sporadic, unquantified recall dominated by hallucinated titles or random numeric fields, and the authors concluded it was not practical enough to warrant a quantitative table.
core_contribution: The paper (Colacicco, Guida, Di Palma, Narducci, Di Noia; Politecnico di Bari; SmaLLEXT@CIKM'25 workshop) extends a prior preliminary study (Di Palma et al., SIGIR 2025) that found MovieLens-1M is memorized by LLaMA and OpenAI model families using only manual prompt engineering. This paper's own contribution is to systematically compare three families of memorization-detection/extraction techniques on MovieLens-1M with LLaMA-1B and LLaMA-3B: (i) jailbreak prompt engineering (white-box, adapting a Context Compliance Attack template from Russinovich and Salem), (ii) unsupervised latent knowledge discovery via Contrast-Consistent Search (CCS, Burns et al.) and Cluster-Norm (Laurito et al.), probing internal activations to classify true vs. fabricated dataset statements, and (iii) Automatic Prompt Engineering (APE, Zhou et al.), adapted as an iterative meta-learning loop that generates, evaluates (via exact-match against Di Palma et al.'s coverage metric), and refines extraction prompts, including a temperature sweep from 0.1 to 2.0. The authors' own empirical finding across all three methods is that APE is the most effective extraction strategy, jailbreak prompting is unreliable, and CCS/Cluster-Norm reveal a latent 'knows vs. doesn't know' signal for textual movie titles but not for numerical user/rating fields.
threat_model: This is not an attacker-vs-defender security paper with an explicit adversary and defense; it is a privacy/memorization auditing study framed around Membership Inference Attacks (MIA) as background (Shokri et al. 2017, Nasr et al. 2019 are cited as prior work, not the paper's own method). The paper's own setting: a researcher/auditor with either black-box query access (manual/jailbreak prompting, APE) or white-box access to hidden activations (CCS, Cluster-Norm) attempts to extract or detect memorized MovieLens-1M records (items, users, ratings) from LLaMA-1B/3B models that were presumably trained or fine-tuned on data overlapping with this benchmark. The practical concern motivating the work is that MovieLens-1M is a widely used RecSys benchmark, so if it is memorized, standard train/test evaluation protocols for LLM-based recommenders become unreliable (test-set leakage) rather than a malicious-exfiltration threat model per se.
limitations: Explicitly acknowledged by the authors: (1) for CCS/Cluster-Norm, the high accuracy on movie titles is ambiguous -- it may reflect the model distinguishing genuine from synthetic-sounding titles via general linguistic plausibility rather than true memorization, and the authors state 'it is unclear whether the learned features should be interpreted as memorization,' concluding that only next-token prediction on raw data reliably confirms memorization; (2) APE and CCS both fail on numerical user/rating fields, which the authors hypothesize stems from tokenizer effects that strip alphanumeric IDs of coherent semantic structure -- this is stated as a hypothesis, not verified; (3) experiments are restricted to MovieLens-1M and LLaMA-1B/3B only (chosen specifically to enable white-box activation access and comparability with the SIGIR 2025 baseline), so generalization to other datasets, other model families (e.g., GPT), or larger LLaMA variants (70B) is untested and left as future work; (4) the jailbreak arm produced no quantitative results, only a qualitative negative conclusion, which weakens the rigor of the cross-method comparison for that arm; (5) this is a 4-page workshop paper (SmaLLEXT@CIKM'25) with limited experimental scale (single dataset, two small LLaMA sizes, no statistical significance testing reported).

datasets_used: MovieLens-1M (users.dat: 6,040 records/5 features; movies.dat: 3,952 records/3 features; ratings.dat: 1,000,209 records/4 features)
quantitative_results: CCS balanced accuracy: movies.dat 0.92, users.dat 0.51, ratings.dat 0.53 (random baseline 0.50)
Cluster-Norm balanced accuracy: movies.dat 0.94, users.dat 0.52, ratings.dat 0.51 (random baseline 0.50)
APE item exact-match, LLaMA-3B by temperature: 7% (T=0.1), 16% (T=0.5), 26% (T=0.7, peak), 25% (T=0.9), 1% (T=1.2), 0.2% (T=2.0)
APE user and rating exact-match: below 0.1% ('-') for both LLaMA-1B and LLaMA-3B at all six temperatures tested
baselines_compared: Di Palma et al. (2025, SIGIR) manual-prompting memorization coverage baseline reproduced in Table 4: 1.93% item / 10.98% user / 6.49% rating (LLaMA-1B-scale) and 2.68% item / 13.26% user / 6.22% rating (LLaMA-3B-scale)
Random-guessing baseline (0.50 balanced accuracy) for CCS/Cluster-Norm probes
